title: System Administrator
metaTitle: System Administrator Resume: Tips & Examples for 2026
metaDescription: Land your next sysadmin role with a resume that highlights infrastructure, security, and automation skills. See real examples and what hiring managers want.
h1: System Administrator Resume Guide: Skills, Examples & ATS Tips
intro: System administrators keep the infrastructure running—and hiring managers want to see proof you can handle uptime, security patches, and disaster recovery. This guide shows you how to translate your hands-on experience into a resume that gets past both ATS systems and human eyes.
audience: Early-career sysadmins, IT support specialists moving into infrastructure roles, and career switchers with technical certifications like CompTIA or Linux looking to land their first dedicated admin position.

topSkills.name: Windows Server & Active Directory
topSkills.why: Most enterprise environments still run on Windows Server; demonstrating AD management, group policy, and user provisioning is table stakes.

topSkills.name: Linux Administration
topSkills.why: Critical for cloud, DevOps-adjacent, and open-source environments; familiarity with Ubuntu, CentOS, or RHEL is increasingly expected.

topSkills.name: Cloud Infrastructure (AWS, Azure, GCP)
topSkills.why: Hybrid and cloud-first deployments dominate; admins need to manage EC2 instances, VPCs, virtual networks, and storage.

topSkills.name: Virtualization (VMware, Hyper-V)
topSkills.why: Most data centers and mid-market companies use virtualization; experience with hypervisors and VM management is highly valued.

topSkills.name: Network Administration
topSkills.why: Routers, switches, DNS, DHCP, and VLANs are core responsibilities; networking fundamentals show you understand infrastructure holistically.

topSkills.name: Security & Access Control
topSkills.why: Patching, firewalls, SSL certificates, encryption, and user permission audits are non-negotiable in a security-conscious world.

topSkills.name: Monitoring & Logging (Nagios, Prometheus, ELK Stack)
topSkills.why: Proactive monitoring and alerting reduce downtime; familiarity with observability tools signals operational maturity.

topSkills.name: Scripting & Automation (PowerShell, Bash, Python)
topSkills.why: Repetitive tasks should be automated; scripting skills separate proactive admins from reactive ticket-closers.

topSkills.name: Backup & Disaster Recovery
topSkills.why: Business continuity is mission-critical; admins who document and test RTO/RPO requirements are invaluable.

topSkills.name: Ticketing & ITSM (Jira, ServiceNow, Zendesk)
topSkills.why: You'll use these tools daily; showing familiarity with request tracking and SLA management proves you work at scale.

sampleBullets.weak: Managed Windows Server infrastructure and maintained network stability.
sampleBullets.strong: Administered 80+ Windows Server instances across three data centers; reduced unplanned downtime by 35% through proactive patching and monitoring via Nagios.
sampleBullets.lesson: Added specific scale (80+ servers, 3 DCs), quantified impact (35% downtime reduction), and named the tools you used—all signals of hands-on credibility.

sampleBullets.weak: Implemented security measures and updated access controls as needed.
sampleBullets.strong: Audited and migrated 500+ user accounts to Active Directory; enforced least-privilege permissions and reduced security audit findings by 60% within 6 months.
sampleBullets.lesson: Replaced vague 'as needed' with concrete numbers (500+ accounts, 60% improvement) and a timeline, showing scope and measurable outcome.

sampleBullets.weak: Set up cloud services and managed cloud resources.
sampleBullets.strong: Architected and deployed hybrid cloud infrastructure on AWS (EC2, RDS, S3); automated infrastructure provisioning using Terraform, reducing server deployment time from 4 hours to 15 minutes.
sampleBullets.lesson: Specified the platform (AWS), the services (EC2, RDS, S3), the tool (Terraform), and the before/after metric—proving you drove efficiency gains.

commonMistakes.mistake: Listing vague 'IT Support' or 'General Maintenance' without specifics.
commonMistakes.fix: Always name the system, tool, or platform (e.g., 'Managed Hyper-V cluster' or 'Patched 120 Windows Server nodes'), and tie it to a business outcome like uptime, cost, or security.

commonMistakes.mistake: No mention of automation or scripting, even if you've done it.
commonMistakes.fix: Highlight every script or automation you've built—whether it's a PowerShell script for bulk user creation or a Bash cron job for log rotation—to show you save time and reduce human error.
Create a dedicated 'Certifications' section and list the full name and year earned (e.g., 'CompTIA Security+ (2023)', 'AZ-900 Azure Fundamentals (2024)'); recruiters often filter by cert.

commonMistakes.mistake: No disaster recovery or business continuity accomplishments.
commonMistakes.fix: If you've tested backups, built a DR plan, or maintained SLAs, say so with metrics: 'Designed and tested RTO/RPO plan achieving 4-hour RTO and 1-hour RPO; zero data loss in three years.'

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring soft skills like documentation, communication, or on-call experience.
commonMistakes.fix: Admins are on-call and mentors; mention incident response, documentation standards you established, and junior staff you've trained to show leadership.

structureTips: Lead with a professional summary or headline that anchors one flagship platform (e.g., 'Windows Server & Azure Infrastructure Admin with 4 years scaling hybrid cloud environments') to immediately signal fit.
Place 'Certifications' in the top third if you have relevant credentials (Microsoft, CompTIA, Linux, AWS)—they're quick ATS keywords and often requirements for screening.

Group technical skills into categories (e.g., 'Operating Systems: Windows Server 2019/2022, CentOS 7/8, Ubuntu 20.04 LTS'; 'Cloud: AWS, Azure'; 'Tools: Terraform, Ansible, PowerShell'); this helps ATS parsing and readability.

List your most complex or high-impact project first in each role—e.g., 'Migrated 500+ users to cloud-hosted Active Directory' before routine maintenance duties, so recruiters see scale and skill instantly.
salarySignal: Entry-level sysadmin roles in the US typically range from $50,000–$65,000; mid-level positions (3–5 years) range from $70,000–$95,000; senior/lead roles reach $100,000+. Remote and cloud-heavy roles often command premiums in high-cost-of-living areas.

faq.q: What certifications should I list on my sysadmin resume?
faq.a: Prioritize Microsoft (MCSA, MCSE), CompTIA (A+, Security+, CySA+), Linux (LPIC, Red Hat), or cloud certs (AWS Solutions Architect, AZ-900). List them in a dedicated 'Certifications' section with the full name and year earned. Recruiters and ATS systems scan for these specifically.

faq.q: How do I show I'm strong in automation if I haven't scripted much?
faq.a: Start small—any repeated task you've automated counts. Write a bullet about it, even if it's a simple PowerShell loop or Bash script. Then highlight your willingness to learn by mentioning online courses (Coursera, Linux Academy) or personal projects on GitHub if you have them.

faq.q: Should I list every tool I've used, even if I'm not an expert?
faq.a: No—focus on depth over breadth. Highlight tools you've used in production or for meaningful projects (e.g., 'managed Nagios monitoring across 50+ servers'). For lesser tools, group them generically (e.g., 'Familiar with ServiceNow, Jira, and Zendesk ticketing systems') to avoid raising questions.

faq.q: How do I explain gaps in my uptime or security record?
faq.a: Don't shy away; instead, reframe proactively. E.g., 'Inherited unstable infrastructure; implemented new monitoring and patching standards, reducing unplanned downtime by 45%' shows problem-solving and improvement, not failure.

faq.q: What metrics matter most to sysadmin hiring managers?
faq.a: Uptime percentage (99.9%, 99.99%), number of systems managed, deployment or patching time reduction, ticket resolution time, user count, and cost savings (cloud optimization, licensing). Pair every major accomplishment with a number.
